summary: The Goshen Township Board of Zoning Appeals held a public hearing on Case BZA 2024-001, an appeal by Tom Mackey of the zoning inspector's denial of his application to place two 20-foot shipping containers as permanent accessory storage structures on his property at 6873 Westcourt Lane. Township staff argued shipping containers are not customarily found as residential accessory structures and more closely match the definition of temporary portable storage units. After testimony from the applicant, supporters, and a neutral neighbor, the board voted to reverse the administrative denial and approve the appeal with stipulations including painting all containers gray, placing them on gravel pads, and completing all work within 120 days.
decisions: Approved video minutes from January 22nd meeting
Nominated and approved new secretary for the BZA board

Approved appeal BZA 2024-001 reversing the administrative denial of two shipping containers as accessory structures, with stipulations (amended site plan, painting containers gray, gravel pads, 120-day completion deadline)
action_items: Tom Mackey must paint all three shipping containers solid gray per letter of intent
Tom Mackey must install gravel pads before placing new containers
Tom Mackey must complete all work and have it inspected within 120 days of application issuing
Tom Mackey must file the amended site plan with updated setbacks as the official site plan
Township staff to send email about March 11th zoning workshop
Township to recruit alternate members for BZA and Zoning Commission boards
key_amounts: Two 160-square-foot shipping containers proposed (each 20 feet, 9'6" height)
$200 extra cost mentioned by applicant for BZA appeal process
5,000 pounds approximate weight per container mentioned by supporter
120-day deadline for completion of all work
notable_topics: Appeal of zoning inspector denial of shipping containers as permanent residential accessory structures
Whether shipping containers qualify as customary accessory structures under township zoning resolution
Distinction between temporary portable storage units and permanent accessory structures in zoning code
Township zoning resolution lacking specific language addressing shipping containers
Procedural concerns about applicants being denied even the ability to apply for shipping container permits
Property maintenance and neighborhood aesthetics concerns
Nomination of new BZA secretary to replace Thomas Curry
Upcoming zoning workshop on proposed text amendments scheduled for March 11th
notes: Mr. Holmes was absent. The applicant raised procedural concerns that the zoning department had been turning away applicants for shipping container permits without accepting formal applications, meaning there is no record of past denials. Robert Seifried, who appears to be a former zoning official, stated he had issued a shipping container permit to the applicant in 2018 and criticized the current administrator. The property at 6873 Westcourt Lane is legally owned by Billy and Lila Mackie (applicant's parents) though Tom Mackey considers it his property via early inheritance. The house on the property is uninhabitable. The applicant already has one existing shipping container on the property since 2018. The board's approval effectively sets precedent that shipping containers can be considered accessory structures in the township.
title: BZA 2-26-24
